sentences.text: Yes.
In Sachsenhausen, when I was taken to-- to the hospital-- that was at our arrival-- I went to said goodbye to my father, and I never saw him again.
And I don't even know, till today, where he died.
That was the last time I saw him.
I saw my name on a plaque on a big red brick building, which turned out to be a silo.
There were-- not beds-- they're the whatever you call it.
And we were located there, and we used to work in the city.
The thing was that the British used to bomb, bomb Hamburg at night, and the Americans used to bomb them in the daytime.
So forever wherever we were not at work, we had to leave everything, and then run to the basement there.
And that was going on a day in, and day out, and of course, it was very easy to run away there at the time, but they had no place to run.
So we used the work.
We used to-- they used to bomb out the rails, so we had to repair that.
They used to bomb out storage magazines.
We used to work there.
And it was always menial, hard work.
It was so hard that by the end of my time in Hamburg, I could hardly walk already.
From Hamburg, we were taken to Sandbostel.
They were taking-- cleaning out Hamburg because the bombing was-- they couldn't manage anymore.
And that trip took six days.
And the distance weren't far.
But we used to stay and stay on the side rails.
And at that, time many, many people died on the trip.
The trip was about six days.
That was the last trip already, the last transport that we went to Sandbostel.
Sandbostel wasn't really a concentration camp.
That was a camp of prisoners of war.
But because they didn't know what to do with us, so they separated the camp in half.
On one side was to prisoners of war.
On the other side was the concentration camp people.
We arrived there, and it was already almost the end of the war.
We knew already the end was coming.
And behind the fence, one of the fences, there was a camp of Polish officers.
They looked like they came out of a Brooks Brother magazine-- beautifully dressed, and their uniforms, shiny boots.
And they were getting an awful lot of food and everything else from the Red Cross.
Of course, everybody-- there was very few Jews already in my group because somehow, I got-- the last couple months, I got separated from them.
And I was that much better for it.
But they still had my name, and of course, the Polish officers were asking, where you from?
Where you from?
Where you going?
And I found one officer was from my hometown, and he asked me, what's your name?
If I had told him my name, he would've known I was Jewish.
So on the spot, I thought of a Polish name.
I told him, and of course, he gave me some bread, and he gave me chocolate and a few cigarettes.
And every day, for a few days, he was giving me things.
And this way-- What was the Polish name you told him?
My name was Szpakowski, Marion Szpakowski.
That's S-Z-P-A-K-O-W-S-K-I.
About May 1-- I don't exactly remember the date.
Could have been April 31 or May 1.
The British army walked into camp.
We stayed there for a bit, a day or two.
We didn't get any food from them, even less than from the Germans.
And of course, there was a lot of Poles and Russians in the camp.
They were in pretty good shape, physically.
They went through the villages, and they used to rob the Germans of food.
I-- being very hungry, I went to the village too.
And I thought, I'll get some food.
But, being a schlimazel, me and about 30 other guys like me were caught again by the Germans that must have come back to a to the village to sort through the town.
We were caught.
There was not SS anymore.
It was just a soldiers, the Whermacht, the army.
But the civilian population was complaining that we are robbing them, so they took us back into prison or whatever.
They didn't know what to do with us.
They had no place for us, so they turned us over to the civilian authorities.
Civilian authorities didn't know what to do with us, so they were taking us from village to village, from town to town, and that's how I worked.
From May 1 to May 6, I was walking, and walking, and walking, and all 30 guys of us.
Then, the night of May 6, I decided with another fellow, with another Polack, to run away.
And we weren't really watched anymore.
And we ran away, and we were going towards the front.
The front we knew was very close because you could hear the shooting and the guns very clearly.
And as we were passing by-- must have been headquarters of German Army yet-- the guy on the guards, the guard soldier stopped us.
Where you going?
What are you doing?
And he put us back in, took us back into prison.
And took us to a farm.
They put us in a barn full of German soldiers.
And they put us in a-- in a straw there.
It was full of-- just the way the German soldiers were sleeping.
We were sleeping in the straw with them.
I don't even remember we got any food or we didn't get any food.
Anyway, the second night, when we got up in the morning, was May 8.
There wasn't a soldier around, was nobody around.
I saw a lady walking into the barn doing something.
I didn't know who she was so much, but I said to her, Frau, Frau.
I was burning up with temperature, with fever, I wanted a little something, hot water or something to drink.
So she comes over, and she starts talking.
I noticed right away that she's not German.
She was either Polish or Ukrainian, a worker on the farm.
And she said, the war is over.
That's it, no more war.
Well anyway, we went with her.
We got some food.
And they took us to a farm, a different farm, where there was a collection of farm workers-- people, Poles and Ukrainians and Russians, some of them, that were working for the civilian population.
They weren't in concentration camps.
They were just farm workers.
And they were drinking, and they were eating, and they were singing.
And I was laying in bed, half dead.
So they found a doctor someplace, and they took me to a hospital, to a civilian hospital in Germany, in Bremerhaven, which we must have been close to Bremerhaven.
In Bremerhaven, I was admitted to the hospital.
They took me into a room, and there was about six or seven other guys like me.
Not one off them Jews, and I already kept my Polish name.
The reason for it was, I didn't know if there was any Jews left, whether any Jews survived.
I haven't seen as Jew.
I haven't heard from anybody.
And I stuck with my Polish name and my Polish nationality.
And nobody knew I was a Jew.
We were-- I was in the hospital for about three months, and then I was released.
They released me.
I said, I have no clothes.
So they let me go in a hospital pajama.
They had nothing else.
I went to city hall.
The city hall says, we have nothing.
We have-- the only thing we have is a navy-- German navy uniform.
I said give me German navy uniform.
I can't walk around in pajamas.
They gave me that uniform.
They also gave me a address to a civilian party, to a family that wanted to rent out a room.
They needed-- they gave me a few marks, and they needed the extra income.
And I lived there for a little while.
At that time there was a curfew for the Germans till 10 o'clock. 10 o'clock-- or well, I skipped something yet before I should've mention.
No, that comes later.
That's true.
One night I was walking on the street after 10 o'clock in the German navy uniform.
And a few Americans soldiers grabbed me.
They wanted to beat me up.
They thought I was a German.
That was-- there were still hot after the war, you know?
So I started talking to them in Yiddish, and they were a few Jewish boys in American army.
But of course, they didn't believe me I was Jewish.
They took me to their captain.
And the captain talked to me.
Of course, I didn't know any English, so I spoke Yiddish to them.
And he says to me, if you're really Jewish, Friday night we have services in the Red Cross building, there someplace.
If you're really Jewish, come to the services.
I came to the services, and I got acquainted with a few Jewish soldiers.
They were stationed in the hospital, and that hospital where I was converted to American army hospital.
And they took me in.
They gave me an American uniform, and I lived with them.
And that's for the first time, I lived like a king.
Food, there was a mess hall.
There wasn't-- there's maybe 20, 30 soldiers stationed there.
We had a beautiful dining room.
We had bathrooms.
We had-- anything you can desire, I had.
But of course, I went to work.
I went to work in American PX.
I worked in American PX.
There was a-- the man in charge, I don't remember his name.
His name was Julius, was a Jewish captain.
He took me in, and the first things he put me is to wash the floor.
And he introduced me to a guy, was a German working there doing the same thing.
He was an engineer that before the war was building roads in Russia, and he spoke perfect Russian.
So of course, I could communicate with him, and we got very friendly.
He was a very nice gentleman, elderly gentleman.
To me at that time, I don't know how old was.
I was 20 years old, so to me he was older.
And, I lived in that hospital, and I walked the streets of Bremerhaven evenings, and I heard some Polish speaking, so I got acquainted with a few guys.
They were working on a Polish ship.
They were sailors on a Polish ship.
One day, they came and they said, one guy got sick from the crew, would you like to work on the ship?
I says, of course.
What do I have?
I have nothing to lose.
I have nothing.
And they took me to the central intelligence agency.
I got permission to leave, and I started working on the ship.
It was a Polish ship, American owned ship, chartered to the Polish government.
That ship, at my time, never went to Poland because the Americans said to the new Polish government-- the communist after the war-- either you buy the ship or return it.
They evidently had no money to buy the ship, so they returned to ship to America.
In the meantime, we were sailing between England and Germany every week.
Used to bring stores from England for the American army.
And we had a life on the ship like you couldn't imagine.
And then when they had to return the ship, I went with the whole crew to America.
We landed in Hoboken, I remember, sometimes in the spring of 1946.
And I sailed.
The ship was SS Kolno.
K-O-L-N-O.
There was five of those sister ships that were chartered or lent to the Polish government that had to be returned.
It wasn't a very big ship, but it crossed the Atlantic ocean just like on a submarine.
[LAUGHS] It was really frightful because it was really a small ship.
In Hoboken, we went to New York and met some people first.
We went to the Polish National Home.
That's the only place I knew.
We didn't know anything about New York or anything.
Some of our people spoke already a little English because they were in England through the war, but I didn't.

And as a matter of fact, the first night I went to New York, took me a whole-- a whole night to get back to the ship because without speaking English, in the subways, everybody shows you different way.
But anyway, that's not important.
And we sailed on that ship for quite a few months.
We went to Canada.
We went to Cuba.
We went to Nova Scotia, to Halifax, and Jamaica, where I contracted malaria, and I was in the hospital there.
Then I came back, I was in a hospital here, Marine hospital, Staten Island.
And then came the day where I had to be repatriated to Poland, and I said, I'm not going.
As a seaman, you have permission to stay here 29 days, any nationality.
You get off the ship, you have official permission for 29 days.
Anyway I started-- I took a test from the Coast Guard, and I got a Coast Guard card, permission to work on American ships.
Then I had to go through the American unions because that's the only way you could get on a ship.
Well anyway, I got on a ship, and we sailed, went to Germany.
In Germany I got off the ship.
I got a permission-- three day pass, and I went-- near Munich was a camp.
In the meantime, I found out some of my family survived, and they were in a DP camp in Foehrenwald.
I got a pass for three days, and I surprised them.
They didn't know anything about-- they knew already I was alive, but they had no idea I was coming, and I came from America.
It was a big deal.
It was wonderful to see them.
It was my uncle, aunt, and two children, a boy and a girl.
My aunt was my mother system.
Her name was Dora.
And my uncle was Bernard.
Later they emigrated to Israel.

While I was doing research, trying through the Red Cross, International Red Cross, and through other organizations, I found out that my sister was taken together with 2,000 women-- Jewish women-- on a ship on the Baltic sea, and sunk, purposely.
That was our very civilized and cultured Germany, which I have to argue with everybody till today because some people have very short memories.
Where my father died, I don't know, could never find out.
But it had to be between January 1 of '45 and the end of the war, which was only four months.
After the three day pass, I came back to the ship, and I kept sailing.
I was sailing.
Then in 1949, I signed up as a crew member on the first Israeli ship that Israeli-- Israel bought here.
And the crew here, with the Israeli captain, took the ship to Israel.
In Israel, I was about two weeks, and there I met my family that just arrived from Israel-- from Germany from the DP camp.
I looked at them up, and they lived in a very poor conditions there.
That was right after the '48 war-- the war of survival.
And the conditions were very rough there.
They lived like in tents.
They had no place to cook there, no place to wash or anything.
But because my cousin got married in the DP camp in Germany, which is also a little story, and her husband was a famous soccer player, which was very sought for in Israel.
So he had a little preferences, and he got some kind of apartment, and they moved out after a short while.
But otherwise, they really had it rough there for the first years.
Next, I went back. '49, I came back here.
As a matter of fact, we were shipped back by plane from Israel.
That was in '49.
I-- we stayed in Paris for about three days, and then we came back to New York.
Came back to New York, the immigration didn't like my papers.
They took me to Ellis Island.
I spend there five weeks.
They wanted to deport me.
But luckily a friend of a friends of a friend knew a judge, and the judge signed some kind of paper, and in one day, they released me.
After they released me, I went back to working on ships, on American ships.
And I worked on ships till almost the end of 1951.
In the meantime, I have met a beautiful girl.
We kept-- I met her sometimes in 1947.
When I came back from Germany from the DP camp, I brought regards for her from my cousin, from other people, and that's how we met.
And--
Her name, at the time, was Bianca, which is my wife today.
And we didn't keep company then, but we saw each other quite often between friends or relatives, her relatives and, and-- but then I started spending more time in New York.
And we started going out, which was, I think the first date we had was on her birthday in 1950.
And then we got engaged, and decided to get married.
We got married on December 1, 1951 in Brooklyn.
And that was the end of my sailing career.
That was it.
I never went out after that.
We lived in Brooklyn for a while-- for a while, for five years.
Then we bought a house in Queens.
We moved there.
We had already one child in Brooklyn.
When we moved, he was two years old.
My oldest son is Howard.
My younger one is Ronald.
I was doing the electrical work.
I became an electrician.
That's what I was on ships.
I was a ship's electrician, and then I became electrician over here.
